posts.title: Understanding O. Reg. 406/19: What Ontario Contractors Need to Know
posts.excerpt: Ontario's excess soil regulation continues to evolve. Here's what constructors, developers, and property owners need to know to stay compliant in 2026.
posts.body:
## What is O. Reg. 406/19?

Ontario's On-Site and Excess Soil Management regulation came into force in January 2021, fundamentally changing how excess soil is managed across the province. Whether you're a constructor breaking ground on a new subdivision, a developer managing multiple sites, or a municipality handling road rehabilitation, this regulation almost certainly applies to you.

## Who Does It Apply To?

The regulation applies to any project that generates excess soil — soil that will be removed from the project area. There are two project classes:

- **Class 1 projects** — large-scale construction and infrastructure projects with the most comprehensive documentation requirements
- **Class 2 projects** — smaller projects with a simplified but still mandatory set of requirements

Projects below minimum thresholds may qualify for exemptions, but those thresholds are narrow and project-specific. When in doubt, treat the project as regulated.

## Key Compliance Requirements

- Excess Soil Management Plans (ESMPs) are mandatory for Class 1 projects and must be registered before soil movement begins
- All soil lots must be tracked in the Ontario Excess Soil Registry
- Qualified Persons (QPs) must author and sign all key documents — there is no self-certification path
- Receiving sites must complete an Excess Soil Destination Assessment (ESDA) before accepting any fill
- Reuse standards depend on the receiving site's current and planned use — residential, commercial, and agricultural sites each carry different thresholds

## Common Mistakes That Trigger Compliance Problems

1. **Moving soil before an ESMP is registered** — even a single truckload before registration can trigger enforcement
2. **Using an unqualified consultant** — documents signed by a non-QP are invalid; your project will not pass regulatory review
3. **Failing to register receiving sites** — unregistered receiving sites cannot legally accept regulated fill
4. **Incomplete chain-of-custody documentation** — hauling records must be maintained throughout the project and retained for a prescribed period
5. **Misclassifying project scale** — underestimating the volume of excess soil to avoid Class 1 requirements is one of the most common (and costly) errors we see

## What Has Changed Since 2021?

The Ministry of the Environment, Conservation and Parks (MECP) has continued to issue guidance and clarifications since the regulation came into force. Key updates have tightened the definition of "project area," expanded registry requirements, and clarified QP liability. Staying current with MECP guidance is not optional — it is part of what your QP should be doing on your behalf.

## How XS Soil Solutions Can Help

Our Qualified Persons provide compliance reviews, ESMP preparation, registry submissions, and full project management from first soil movement to final documentation. Not sure if your project is compliant? [Contact us](mailto:info@xssoil.ca) for a free project assessment or call **905-334-1197**.

posts.title: What Is a Qualified Person (QP) — and Why Your Project Needs One
posts.excerpt: Ontario's excess soil regulation requires a licensed Qualified Person on virtually every regulated project. Here's what the QP designation means, who qualifies, and why it matters for your compliance outcomes.
posts.body:
## The QP Requirement Is Not Optional

Under O. Reg. 406/19, a Qualified Person (QP) must prepare, certify, and take professional responsibility for the key documents that govern how excess soil is managed on your project. There is no workaround, no exemption for smaller firms, and no grace period.

Yet despite this, we continue to see projects where ESMPs and site assessments have been prepared by individuals who do not meet the regulatory definition of a QP — putting clients at legal and financial risk.

## Who Qualifies as a QP Under the Regulation?

Ontario's excess soil regulation defines a Qualified Person as a member of one of the following regulated professions who holds the competencies required to perform the specific work:

- **Professional Engineers (P.Eng.)** licensed by Professional Engineers Ontario
- **Professional Geoscientists (P.Geo.)** licensed by Professional Geoscientists Ontario
- **Ontario Land Surveyors** in limited applicable contexts

Holding the credential alone is not sufficient. The QP must also have the specific competencies — environmental site assessment, soil characterization, regulatory interpretation — relevant to the work being certified. Hiring a structural P.Eng. to sign an ESMP does not meet the standard.

## What Does the QP Do on a Regulated Project?

The QP's role spans the entire project lifecycle:

- **Pre-project:** Review site history, identify potential contamination, determine project class and applicable requirements
- **Planning:** Prepare the Excess Soil Management Plan and register it in the Ontario Excess Soil Registry before soil movement begins
- **During construction:** Oversee soil characterization sampling, review lab results, authorize soil lots for receiving sites
- **Post-project:** Certify final documentation, ensure registry records are complete and accurate

The QP signs off on compliance at each stage. Their professional licence is on the line — which is why experienced QPs take this work seriously and why you should care about who yours is.

## The Risk of Getting It Wrong

Documents signed by a non-QP, or a QP without the relevant competency, are legally invalid. Receiving sites that accept fill based on invalid documentation are themselves in violation. We have seen projects where non-compliant soil movement resulted in enforcement action, stop-work orders, and costly remediation requirements — all of which could have been avoided with proper oversight from the start.

## XS Soil Solutions' QP Commitment

Every XS Soil Solutions engagement is led by a licensed QP from day one. We don't assign QP sign-off to junior staff or use it as a rubber stamp — the QP who reviews your project is the same professional who is accountable for the work. If you have questions about whether your current project has proper QP coverage, [get in touch](mailto:info@xssoil.ca).

posts.title: Soil Reuse vs. Landfill Disposal: The True Cost Comparison
posts.excerpt: Landfill tipping fees have risen sharply across Ontario. With proper planning, most excess soil qualifies for beneficial reuse — saving projects tens of thousands of dollars. Here's how the math works.
posts.body:
## The Default Is the Expensive Option

For decades, the default approach to excess soil on Ontario construction sites was simple: truck it out, tip it at a licensed facility, and move on. That model worked when tipping fees were low and regulatory oversight was minimal. Neither of those conditions still applies.

Landfill tipping fees across Ontario have increased significantly over the past decade. In many parts of the GTA, fees for non-hazardous fill now exceed $80–$120 per tonne, with fuel surcharges and haulage costs layered on top. A mid-sized urban development generating 5,000 tonnes of excess soil can face $600,000 or more in disposal costs — before the project has produced a single saleable unit.

## What Is Beneficial Reuse?

O. Reg. 406/19 was designed in part to facilitate the movement of clean excess soil to sites that need it — quarry rehabilitation, brownfield development, road embankments, municipal infrastructure projects — rather than filling up licensed facilities with material that poses no environmental risk.

For soil to qualify for beneficial reuse, it must meet the applicable reuse standard for the receiving site. Standards vary based on how the receiving site is zoned and used:

- **Agricultural or other (Table 1)** — the most conservative thresholds
- **Residential/parkland (Table 2)** — standard suburban receiving site
- **Commercial/industrial (Table 3)** — the least restrictive for non-sensitive land use

Soil characterization — sampling and lab analysis conducted or overseen by a QP — determines which table your soil meets.

## How Much Can Reuse Save?

The numbers vary by project, but the savings are consistently significant. On a typical project:

- **Landfill disposal** (tipping + haulage): $90–$150 per tonne
- **Beneficial reuse** (registry + QP fees): $15–$35 per tonne
- **Savings per tonne**: $55–$115

For a 5,000-tonne project, that's a potential saving of $275,000–$575,000. Even accounting for QP fees, sampling costs, and registry time, reuse planning almost always delivers a strong return.

## Why Projects Still Default to Landfill

Most landfill-bound soil isn't heading there because reuse is impossible — it's heading there because:

1. **No receiving site was lined up early enough** — matching soil to receiving sites takes planning lead time that wasn't built into the schedule
2. **Characterization wasn't done** — without lab data, you can't register soil in the registry or confirm reuse eligibility
3. **The contractor defaulted to a known process** — landfill disposal is familiar; the registry process has a learning curve

All three are solvable with the right expertise applied at the right time.

## Start the Conversation Early

The window for reuse planning is front-loaded. By the time soil is moving, it's often too late to recapture the savings. XS Soil Solutions works with project teams during the pre-construction phase to identify reuse pathways, line up receiving sites, and structure the documentation so that beneficial reuse is the default — not the fallback. [Talk to us](mailto:info@xssoil.ca) about your upcoming project.

posts.title: How Long Does an Excess Soil Management Plan Actually Take?
posts.excerpt: One of the most common questions we get: how far in advance do we need to engage a QP for ESMP preparation? The answer depends on project complexity — and it's almost always earlier than clients expect.
posts.body:
## The Question We Hear Most Often

"We start moving soil in six weeks — is that enough time to get an ESMP done?"

Sometimes, yes. Often, no. And the difference between those two outcomes almost always comes down to how much groundwork was done before the question was asked.

## What Goes Into an ESMP?

An Excess Soil Management Plan is not a form — it is a regulatory document that must reflect accurate, project-specific information and be signed by a Qualified Person. The key components include:

- **Project description and area definition** — boundaries, volumes, project class determination
- **Site history and Phase 1 ESA review** — required to identify areas of potential environmental concern (APECs)
- **Soil characterization plan** — sampling strategy, number of samples, parameters to be analyzed
- **Receiving site information** — confirmed receiving sites with completed ESDAs or exemptions
- **Excess Soil Registry submission** — electronic filing that must be completed before soil movement

Each component has its own lead time, and they are largely sequential.

## Realistic Timeline Breakdown

- **Phase 1 ESA review** (if not already done): 1–2 weeks
- **Soil characterization sampling and lab analysis**: 2–3 weeks
- **Receiving site identification and ESDA completion**: 2–4 weeks (variable)
- **ESMP drafting and QP review**: 1–2 weeks
- **Registry submission and confirmation**: 3–5 business days
- **Total (typical): 6–10 weeks**

If you're starting from zero — no Phase 1 ESA on file, no receiving sites identified — ten weeks is a reasonable minimum. Projects with complex site histories, large volumes, or multiple receiving sites can take longer.

## What Causes Delays?

In our experience, the most common causes of ESMP delays are:

1. **Lab turnaround time** — standard turnaround is 10–15 business days; rush options exist but cost more
2. **Receiving site readiness** — a receiving site that hasn't completed its ESDA cannot be registered; this is the single most common bottleneck
3. **Incomplete site history documentation** — gaps in the record require additional investigation before the QP can certify
4. **Multiple revision rounds** — first drafts often require client or municipal review that adds time

## What You Can Do to Speed It Up

- Engage a QP **at the design stage**, before contracts are signed
- Identify and pre-qualify receiving sites as early as possible — this is the step most commonly left too late
- Ensure a current Phase 1 ESA is on file before the QP starts
- Consolidate project team decision-making so revision cycles are short

XS Soil Solutions is structured for speed. Our QPs respond same-day, our field teams mobilize within 24–48 hours, and our document workflows are optimized for tight timelines. If you have an upcoming project with a tight soil-movement schedule, [contact us now](mailto:info@xssoil.ca) before your window closes.

posts.title: Excess Soil Compliance for Municipal Infrastructure Projects
posts.excerpt: Municipal road, sewer, and utility projects generate significant volumes of excess soil — and carry the same regulatory obligations as private sector construction. Here's what Ontario municipalities need to understand.
posts.body:
## Municipalities Are Not Exempt

One of the more persistent misconceptions in the sector is that municipal infrastructure projects — road rehabilitation, watermain replacement, stormwater upgrades — operate outside the O. Reg. 406/19 framework. They do not.

Municipalities are subject to the same project class determinations, ESMP requirements, registry obligations, and QP standards as private sector constructors. The only relevant exemptions relate to project scale, not ownership — and most municipal infrastructure projects easily exceed those thresholds.

## What Makes Municipal Projects Different?

While the regulatory obligations are the same, municipal projects have characteristics that require a slightly different planning approach:

**Linear projects** — road and utility corridor projects often span multiple properties and generate soil from several distinct zones with different histories. Characterization sampling strategies need to account for spatial variability across the entire corridor.

**Budget cycles** — municipal capital budgets are set well in advance. ESMP and characterization costs need to be scoped and included in tender documents or project estimates before procurement.

**Procurement requirements** — many municipalities require that QP services be procured through an RFP or vendor-of-record process. Engaging a QP after tender award limits your schedule options.

**Receiving site coordination** — municipalities often have preferred receiving sites (quarry rehabilitation, rural parks, municipal fill sites) but these must complete ESDAs before soil can be placed, which takes time.

## Planning for Compliance in a Municipal Context

The municipalities that handle excess soil compliance most efficiently are those that have built it into their standard project delivery process:

1. **Include ESMP costs in capital estimates** at the project approval stage
2. **Engage QP services through an annual roster or standing offer** rather than project-by-project procurement
3. **Pre-qualify receiving sites** across your municipal portfolio so you have options ready when projects break ground
4. **Build characterization and registry timelines into master schedules** — typically 8–12 weeks before soil movement

## Working With Municipalities

XS Soil Solutions has direct experience supporting Ontario municipalities through excess soil compliance on infrastructure programs. We understand procurement constraints, council report timelines, and the operational realities of public works delivery. Our fixed-fee proposals give budget certainty, and our QPs are available for pre-bid consultations to help municipal staff scope work accurately.

For municipal procurement inquiries or project consultations, contact us at [info@xssoil.ca](mailto:info@xssoil.ca) or **905-334-1197**.
